serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Red Date & White Fungus Soup is approximately 200 grams, often served in a small dessert bowl.

nutrient_source.title: White Fungus with Red Dates Dessert (银耳红枣甜汤)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore, Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match from HPB Foodo database for 'White Fungus with Red Dates Dessert (银耳红枣甜汤)'.

gi_source.title: Estimation based on similar sweetened soups and ingredients
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (for components)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated based on the GI of red dates (jujubes, GI 55) and the sugar content (8g/100g). Analogous to other sweetened dessert soups. White fungus itself has negligible glycemic impact. GI of 60 is a reasonable estimate for a moderately sweetened dessert soup.

hypertension.guidance: This soup is very low in sodium (2 mg per 100 g), making it a suitable dessert choice for individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Due to its moderate sugar content (8 g per 100 g) and medium glycemic index (~60), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this soup in moderation and consider portion control to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: With very low fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and negligible cholesterol, this dessert soup is generally a heart-healthy option for those with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Date & White Fungus Soup (红枣银耳汤, Hong Zao Yin Er Tang) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup widely enjoyed in Singapore and across East and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by simmering dried white fungus (snow fungus, Tremella fuciformis) and red dates (jujubes) in water with rock sugar until the fungus softens to a translucent, jelly-like consistency and the broth develops a delicate natural sweetness. The soup has a light, slightly viscous texture with a subtly sweet and earthy flavour. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (43 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat and sodium, and provides a moderate amount of carbohydrates and sugars. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. In traditional Chinese medicine, it is valued for its purported skin-hydrating and lung-nourishing properties. Clinically, its low sodium content makes it suitable for hypertensive individuals, while its moderate sugar content warrants portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes.
